coredata.dc:title: An effective strategy for the detection of tetracycline by N,S-doped carbon nanodots after preconcentration with a hybrid functional nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Sianglam, Pradthana
Ngamdee, Kessarin
Ittisanronnachai, Somlak
Promarak, Vinich
Ren, Xiang-Kui
Ngeontae, Wittaya
coredata.dc:description: Contamination with the antibiotic tetracycline (TC) used in animal husbandry is a serious problem in the food safety and environmental pollution fields and may cause severe diseases in humans. An effective strategy for detecting tetracycline at trace levels is therefore needed. Here, a combination of two effective strategies for enhancing the analytical performance of tetracycline detection, namely, a sensitive fluorescence method after a preconcentration step, was developed. Ultratrace tetracycline contamination was extracted with a hybrid functional nanocomposite of magnetic nanoparticles coated with a mixed metal hydroxide (MNPs@MMH), which were separated using a permanent magnet and transferred to a small volume of the eluting solution. Through the inner filter effect (IFE), the concentration of eluted tetracycline was effectively correlated with the decrease in emission intensity of the fluorescent probes (N,S-doped carbon nanodots; N,S-CDs). Under the optimal conditions, the detection range for tetracycline was 0.80 to 10 µM. The detection limit for TC sensing was 0.16 µM. This reported method was used to successfully detect tetracycline contamination in milk and water samples from an animal husbandry farm.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) SEM images of MNPs and (c) the EDS spectra of MNPs (d, e) SEM images of MNPs@MMH (f) the EDS spectra of MNPs@MMH (g) TEM image of N,S-CDs, (h) HRTEM image of the N,S-CDs, and (i) FT-IR spectra of cysteamine, lemonade, and the synthesized N,S-CDs.

The overlay of absorption spectra of 5 µM tetracycline, and fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of N,S-CDs (λex/λem = 370/438 nm) without and with 5 µM tetracycline in 0.2 M acetic-acetate buffer pH 4.0.

Fluorescence spectra and the corresponding degrees of fluorescence quenching (F0-F) of N,S-CDs in 0.2 M acetic-acetate buffer at pH 4.0 after the extraction of 10 µM TC in 40 mL of sample solution using different final detection volumes.

(a) Fluorescence spectrum and (b) the degree of fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs in the presence of various possible interference compounds at 10 µM after extraction with MMH@MNPs recorded in 0.2 M acetic-acetate buffer pH 4.0 and Fluorescence spectra of N,S-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of TC (c) with extraction and (d) without extraction with MMH@MNPs in 0.2 M acetic-acetate buffer pH 4.0.
Determines TC concentrations in milk and husbandry farm wastewater samples using the proposed method and HPLC technique (n = 3).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Contamination with the antibiotic tetracycline (TC) used in animal husbandry is a serious problem in the food safety and environmental pollution fields and may cause severe diseases in humans. An effective strategy for detecting tetracycline at trace levels is therefore needed. Here, a combination of two effective strategies for enhancing the analytical performance of tetracycline detection, namely, a sensitive fluorescence method after a preconcentration step, was developed. Ultratrace tetracycline contamination was extracted with a hybrid functional nanocomposite of magnetic nanoparticles coated with a mixed metal hydroxide (MNPs@MMH), which were separated using a permanent magnet and transferred to a small volume of the eluting solution. Through the inner filter effect (IFE), the concentration of eluted tetracycline was effectively correlated with the decrease in emission intensity of the fluorescent probes (N,S-doped carbon nanodots; N,S-CDs). Under the optimal conditions, the detection range for tetracycline was 0.80 to 10 µM. The detection limit for TC sensing was 0.16 µM. This reported method was used to successfully detect tetracycline contamination in milk and water samples from an animal husbandry farm.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The increased food demand is a result of the increasing size of the agriculture industry. In particular, many animal products from the animal industry are needed to satisfy the increasing demands of the food industry. One of the main problems associated with animal husbandry is animal diseases that originate from bacteria. Antibiotic drugs have been introduced into the animal husbandry industry to control and manage animal diseases. Tetracycline antibiotics (TC) are among the most popular antibiotic drugs and have been widely used to cure bacterial infections in animals. However, in addition to the advantages of TC, excess TC from animal husbandry applications always contaminates animal products, which exerts adverse effects on consumers. Moreover, excess TC from animal husbandry may cause water pollution [1] on farms, and it is difficult to degrade naturally. Several approaches have been proposed for the detection of TC. In general, TC-contaminated food samples have mainly been analyzed using high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [2–9]. This technique uses well-established instrumentation that provides excellent sensitivity and specificity. However, HPLC involves the use of complicated and expensive instruments, requires well-trained scientists, and has a high analysis cost. Thus, an efficient, robust, sensitive, and cost-effective method to detect TC at trace levels of contamination is crucial for food and environmental safety concerns.

In recent decades, fluorescence detection has been a common technique for achieving the sensitivity required to identify several substances. The detection of TC based on fluorescence has been widely reported due to the exceptional qualities of this technique, including a simple measurement, rapid analysis time, cost-effectiveness, and operation without a well-trained scientist. Several fluorescent probes have been suggested for monitoring TC [1,10–24]. Among the reported fluorescence probes, carbon dots and their related structures seem to be the most popular types. They are simple to prepare, provide high fluorescence efficiency, are inexpensive and have been used to detect TC with various sensing mechanisms, such as Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [18], photoinduced electron transfer (PET) [10], and the inner filter effect (IFE) [10,17,19–22]. Most TC detection methods using carbon dots as fluorophores are performed in accordance with fluorescence quenching via the IFE process. The broad absorption characteristic of TC overlapped chiefly with the carbon dot excitation and emission spectra. The detection of fluorescence quenching via the IFE phenomenon provides characteristics superior to those of other mechanisms, such as assay sensitivity, no specific interaction between the target analyte and the fluorophore, and no incubation time. However, the detection of TC fluorescence via the IFE mechanism always suffers from the interference of other species or sample matrices with an absorption wavelength in the same range as that of TC. Thus, if this limitation is overcome, fluorescence detection via IFE will be an excellent strategy to fabricate an efficient assay.

The simple method for overcoming the drawbacks of TC detection via the IFE process is to separate TC from the matrix and transfer it to a suitable medium with a smaller volume. This process reduces the possible interfering species and increases the concentration of TC, allowing detection at ultratrace levels. Several reports [8,25–29] have described the extraction and preconcentration of TC with different extractant materials prior to detection with chromatographic techniques. Furthermore, the layered double hydroxide (LDH) components were used to extract TC [30]. This extraction was based on the fact that TC exists in a negatively charged form in a basic solution, which undergoes ion exchange with the LDH components or mixed metal hydroxide (MMH) adsorbed on the positively charged surface. This type of material possesses exciting characteristics, such as the ability to be easily generated in situ, and the sorbent does not need to be synthesized prior to use (no consumption of energy associated with the preparation). In addition, no toxic organic solvent is needed, and the cost is low. Furthermore, this type of sorbent quickly elutes TC by dissolving it in a small volume of acid, which facilitates the preconcentration process. However, extraction by the LDH component (MMH) requires an inconvenient phase separation step. The LDH phase must be separated from the supernatant by centrifugation.

Magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) have been introduced into modern sample preparation techniques due to their unique intrinsic properties. Most research on MNPs in sample preparation involves surface modification via both chemical and physical processes to achieve suitable surface properties that will extract the target analytes [28,31–41]. However, a few studies have reported the preparation of hybrid materials with MNPs in the sample preparation approach [42–50]. The hybridization between MNPs and LDHs or MMH may overcome the limitations of both materials in the sample preparation approach. The MMH and MNPs hybrid materials extract the anionic species from the sample medium and quickly separate them upon exposure to an external magnetic field without any centrifugation step. This approach seems to be a new and elegant strategy for the preconcentration of TC.

In this context, we aimed to develop a new and efficient direction for TC detection by combining a simple preconcentration step with indirect fluorescence detection. The nanocomposite of MMH-incorporated MNPs was chosen as an eco-friendly extractant for solid-phase dispersion-based extraction of trace levels of TC. The MNPs facilitated the separation of the extractant with extracted TC from the sample solutions using a permanent external magnet, and then TC was easily eluted from the extractant by simply adding a small volume of acetic acid to dissolve the MMH component. The concentration of the concentrated TC was indirectly quantified by estimating the IFE phenomenon or the decrease in fluorescence intensity of the N,S-doped carbon nanodots (N,S-CDs), which were successfully synthesized using the hydrothermal reaction of lemonade and cysteamine. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching induced by adding TC was elucidated using several techniques. In addition, the parameters that may affect the extraction efficiency and the fluorescence signal were investigated in detail and optimized. Furthermore, the applicability of the proposed strategy in actual sample analysis was evaluated by applying it to the milk and wastewater obtained from an animal husbandry farm.

AR-grade substances were utilized immediately, without purification. Aluminum chloride (AlCl3), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), and ammonium hydroxide (28 % NH4OH) were purchased from QRec (New Zealand). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and ferrous sulfate (FeSO4) were received from Carlo Erba Reagents (Italy). Acetic acid (CH3COOH) was purchased from RCI Labscan. Limes were obtained from the Non-Muang vegetable market. Ferric chloride (FeCl3) was obtained from Riedel-de Haën (Germany). Cysteamine and tetracycline hydrochloride were received from Sigma–Aldrich (USA). Ultrapure water from a RiOsTM Type I Simplicity 185 system (Millipore water) with a specific resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm was used to prepare aqueous solutions. The N,S-CDs and MNPs were synthesized as described in the electronic supporting information.

An RF-6000PC spectrofluorometer (Shimadzu) was used to record all fluorescence spectra. Both excitation and emission slits utilized the same slit width of 5 nm. The morphology and structure of the MNPs@MMH were analyzed using a Helios NanoLab G3 CX scanning electron microscope (FEI, Netherlands). The TEM images of synthesized N,S-CDs were examined with a TECNAI G2 20 transmission electron microscope (FEI, Netherlands) operating at 200 kV, and bright-field high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were captured with a JEM ARM200F (JEOL, Japan) transmission electron microscope with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. A model FS5 fluorescence lifetime spectrometer (Edinburgh Analytical Instruments) was used to determine the fluorescence lifetimes. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired using a Bruker TENSOR27 FTIR spectrometer. An Agilent HP spectrophotometer was used to measure absorption spectra. Magnetic characteristics were evaluated utilizing a Lake Shore VSM 7403 vibrating sample magnetometer. The pH of the solution was measured using a UB-10 Ultra Basic pH meter (Denver Instrument).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preconcentration of TC by MNPs@MMH and detection with N,S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: MNPs@MMH were prepared by generating MMH on the surface of MNPs. A solution composed of 0.1 M AlCl3 (400 µL), 0.3 M MgCl2 (400 µL), 1 M NaOH (500 µL), and an MNPs suspension (300 µL) was added to 40 mL of a TC-containing sample solution. The suspension was then thoroughly shaken by vortexing to achieve extraction. The MNPs@MMH containing TC were then collected using a permanent magnet. The MNPs@MMH containing TC were eluted with 300 µL of 2 M acetic acid and resuspended in 2.5 mL of the solution. The solution was adjusted to pH 4.0 with 0.2 M acetic acetate buffer to obtain the TC solution. The synthesized N,S-CDs (100 µL) were then introduced to the testing TC solution. The mixed solution was incubated at room temperature for 10 min before the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm.

The concentration of TC in the samples, including milk and husbandry farm wastewater, was determined. The husbandry farm wastewater sample was collected from an animal husbandry farm in Khon Kaen Province, Thailand. The sample was filtered with filter paper before detection using the proposed method. The milk sample was obtained from a grocery store in Khon Kaen Province, Thailand. The fat and protein in the milk sample were removed by adding 2.00 mL of 1 % acetic acid in MeOH to 10.00 mL of the milk sample. The mixed solution was vortexed for 2 min and centrifuged at 5000 rpm for 10 min. Before analysis with the suggested approach, the supernatant was collected and filtered through filter paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the composite MNPs@MMH
ce:section.ce:para: The structural, crystallization, and magnetic characteristics of MNPs@MMH were examined to confirm that the nanocomposites were generated. The surface morphologies of the synthesized MNPs and the produced MNPs@MMH were studied with SEM. SEM images of the MNPs and MNPs@MMH are presented in Fig. 1 . As observed, the morphology of the MNPs was approximately uniform and spherical, with a diameter of approximately 51.6 ± 4.4 nm, while the surface of the MNPs@MMH nanocomposite showed a sheet-like morphology with a diameter of approximately 57.4 ± 3.2 nm. The results suggested the successful fabrication of MNPs@MMH. Moreover, the energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) results for the MNPs and MNPs@MMH are displayed in Fig. 1(c) and Fig. 1(f), respectively. The EDS spectra indicated that iron (Fe) and oxygen (O) were the two main components of the MNPs, while the MNPs@MMH composite consisted of Fe, O, Al, and Mg, indicating that MMH was deposited on the MNPs.

The crystallization characteristics of MNPs and hybrid MNPs@MMH nanocomposites were investigated using X-ray diffraction (XRD). Fig. S1 shows the XRD patterns of MNPs and MNPs@MMH. Because of the fine particles, the XRD pattern of MNPs was weak and difficult to identify. However, the MNPs produced diffraction peaks at 2θ = 30.1°, 35.7° and 62.7°, which were related to the 220, 311, and 440 planes of Fe3O4, respectively (JCPDS Card No. 65–3107) [51]. The XRD pattern of MNPs@MMH depicted diffraction peaks at 2θ = 11.4°, 22.6°, 34.4°, 38.8°, 45.4°, 60.6°, and 61.7°, which were attributed to the 003, 006, 009, 012, 018, 110 and 113 planes of MMH, respectively [52]. Moreover, the pattern of MNPs@MMH exhibited a small additional peak at 2θ of 35.7°, corresponding to the 311 basal plane, which is the characteristic peak of magnetite (Fe3O4) or MNPs. However, the other characteristic peaks of MNPs were not observed in the pattern of MNPs@MMH, potentially because the MMH entirely encapsulated the MNPs.

The magnetization properties of the MNPs and MNPs@MMH were analyzed using a vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) at 298 K in a magnetic field ranging from −1000 to 1000 Oe. The hysteresis loops of the MNPs and MNPs@MMH composites were measured, and the results are illustrated in Fig. S2. The saturation magnetization values of the MNPs and MNPs@MMH were 86.48 and 11.60 emu g−1, respectively. The shielding effect of the nonmagnetic MMH waws responsible for the apparent reduction in the magnetization of the MNPs after adding MMH to form MNPs@MMH. This result indicated that the MNPs were encased by MMH, which decreased their magnetism.

Three different batches of MNPs@MMH were prepared and used to determine TC concentrations as a method to determine the reproducibility of the preparation of MNPs@MMH. The results in Fig. S3 showed no significantly different signals between the three different batches of MNPs@MMH, which confirmed the reproducibility of the preparation of MNPs@MMH. Moreover, the TEM image of the synthesized MNPs (Fig. S4) showed an average particle size of 25.3 ± 2.6 nm (N = 106) with an almost spherical shape and narrow size distribution. In addition, the FT-IR spectrum of the MNPs (Fig. S5) exhibited characteristic peaks of Fe3O4 at 3440 cm−1 (OH stretching), 1637 cm−1 (OH bending) and 564 cm−1 (Fe-O).

The fluorescent N,S-CDs were effectively synthesized by performing a simple hydrothermal technique utilizing lemonade and cysteamine as starting ingredients. Absorption and photoluminescence spectra were investigated to assess the optical characteristics of the synthesized N,S-CDs. As shown in Fig. S6(a), the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the N,S-CDs exhibited a characteristic peak at 340 nm, which was attributed to the n → π* transition of CO functionalities [53]. Fig. S6(b) shows the excitation and emission characteristics of the N,S-CDs, indicating well-defined excitation and emission characteristic peaks at 365 and 438 nm, respectively. Interestingly, when the excitation wavelength ranged from 300 to 390 nm, the emission peak remained at 430 nm (Fig. S6(c)), showing that the N,S-CDs may downshift higher-energy UV light to lower-energy visible light [54]. TEM was used to explore the morphology of the N,S-CDs, as shown in Fig. 1(g). The synthesized N,S-CDs presented spherical shapes with a narrow distribution and an average particle size of 3.4 ± 0.5 nm (N = 119). In the HRTEM image of the N,S-CDs (Fig. 1(h)), well-resolved lattice fringes with a spacing of 0.22 nm, which is comparable to the (100) in-plane lattice spacing of graphene, were detected. The significant functionality of the synthesized N,S-CDs was investigated using FT-IR. The FT-IR spectra of cysteamine, lemonade, and the synthesized N,S-CDs are compared in Fig. 1(i). The FT-IR spectra of the N,S-CDs differed from those of the precursors, suggesting the formation of a new compound with different functional groups. The characteristic CO and CO peaks were observed at 1170 cm−1 and 1694 cm−1, which differed from those of their precursor. The stretching vibration of NH in the N,S-CDs was indicated by a tiny peak at 1523 cm−1, confirming the reported amino functionality from the cysteamine precursor. These results confirmed that the successfully synthesized N,S-CDs had remarkable optical and morphological properties and might potentially be used as highly efficient fluorescence probes.

In addition, XPS measurements were also performed to investigate the chemical composition and chemical bonding of the elements in the N,S-CDs nanostructure. The full XPS spectrum of the N,S-CDs revealed five typical peaks at 533.1, 400.7, 284.4, 227.2, and 162.2 eV, which correlated to O1s, N1s, C1s, S2s, and S2p, respectively, as depicted in Fig. S7(a). The high-resolution C1s spectrum in Fig. S7(b) displayed two peaks at 284.786 eV and 287.654 eV that were ascribed to CC/CC and CO/CN, respectively. The O1s spectrum in Fig. S7(c) was deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.803 and 531.183 eV, corresponding to C-OH/COC and CO, respectively. The N1s spectrum, which contained a characteristic peak for the pyridinic/pyrrolic N bond at 399.957 eV, is shown in Fig. S7(d). The S2p spectrum also exhibited a peak at 163.291 eV, corresponding to S2p3/2 (CS) (Fig. S7(e)). According to the aforementioned results, N and S were efficiently doped into the structure of the N,S-CDs. The reproducibility of the preparation of N,S-CDs was determined by comparing the fluorescence spectra of 4 different batches of the synthesized CDs, and the results are shown in Fig. S8. The results confirmed that the N,S-CDs were prepared with high reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TC using the N,S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: TC was detected using the IFE of fluorescent N,S-CDs, and the findings are presented in Fig. 2 . Significant fluorescence emission was observed after excitation of the N,S-CDs at 370 nm. The fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs, on the other hand, was considerably reduced by more than 70 % after only 5 µM TC was added. Based on this result, the fluorescence quenching of the N,S-CDs upon the addition of TC might be used to quantify the concentration of TC indirectly.

Therefore, the quenching mechanism of the N,S-CDs fluorescence induced by TC was elucidated. Fig. 2 shows the absorption characteristics of TC superimposed on the excitation and emission spectra of the N,S-CDs. Thus, fluorescence quenching was induced by the IFE phenomenon. The fluorescence quenching was due to the decrease in the excitation intensity of the light source mediated by partial absorption by TC. Moreover, the emission intensity was also partially absorbed by TC.

The relationship between emission and absorption was examined by varying the TC concentration from 0 to 25 µM to further prove that the fluorescence quenching of the N,S-CDs in the presence of TC caused by the IFE mechanism. The results are illustrated in Fig. S9. The fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs decreased with increasing TC concentration (Fig. S9(a)) and increased TC absorbance (Fig. S9(b)). The decreasing fluorescence intensity (the ratio of the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of TC; F/F0) and the absorbance as a function of TC concentration are compared in Fig. S9(c). Fluorescence quenching was related to the increase in absorbance of TC and confirmed that the decrease in the fluorescence intensity stemmed from the IFE phenomenon.

Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime spectra of the N,S-CDs before and after mixing with TC were investigated. The results in Fig. S10(a) and the fitting parameters in Table S1 show that the N,S-CDs fluorescence lifetime did not change significantly when TC was added. This study revealed that TC and N,S-CDs do not interact. Moreover, the morphology of N,S-CDs in the absence and presence of TC were investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. S10(b) and Fig. S10(c), respectively. The average particle sizes of the N,S-CDs before and after adding TC were 3.40 ± 0.50 nm (N = 119) and 3.35 ± 0.45 nm (N = 121), indicating that no particle aggregation or morphological change occurred when TC was added to the N,S-CDs. Thus, the fluorescence quenching of the N,S-CDs by the TC did not arise from a morphological change. Furthermore, the effect of the excitation wavelength on the decrease in the fluorescence of the N,S-CDs upon adding TC was determined. The results in Fig. S11 show that the % decrease in the fluorescence intensity at each excitation wavelength changed when the excitation wavelength increased from 330 to 400 nm. The maximum % decrease in the fluorescence intensity was observed at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, corresponding to the characteristic absorption of TC. This phenomenon signified that the % decrease in the fluorescence intensity might be related to the absorption characteristic of TC. All of the evidence obtained here suggested that the fluorescence quenching of the N,S-CDs by adding TC indeed was attributed to the IFE mechanism.

The TC concentration was determined by measuring the degree of fluorescence quenching of the N,S-CDs through the IFE phenomenon. Thus, the parameters that may affect the detection sensitivity were optimized, including the pH of the solution and the volume of the N,S-CDs. In addition, the response of the N,S-CDs to other related compounds was studied. The results (Fig. S12 and Fig. S13) are presented and discussed in the electronic supporting information. A pH of 4.0 and a volume of N,S-CDs of 100 μL provided the best detection efficiency. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs was quenched by tetracycline, oxytetracycline and chlortetracycline.

Preconcentration of the trace level of the target analyte is the most powerful strategy for overcoming the limitation of the detection instrument. The concentration can be increased to be sufficiently detected by the instrument, and the sample matrix and possible interference can be removed during the preconcentration step. Thus, we aimed to preconcentrate the TC in the sample solution using the hybrid nanocomposite formed by MMH and MNPs. MMH is an efficient sorbent for TC extraction through ionic interactions between the positive charge of MMH and the negative charge of ionized TC (in alkaline medium). However, this procedure required centrifugation to separate the MMH from the sample solution. Thus, the introduction of MNPs to form a hybrid nanocomposite with MMH was proposed. Notably, the extraction of TC by MNPs is not expected. MNPs are introduced to facilitate the extraction procedure.

For proof of concept, the extraction of 5 µM TC by different adsorbents was compared, and the results are shown in Fig. S14. TC was extracted only when using MMH and MNPs@MMH, as evidenced by the decreasing fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs. On the other hand, TC was not extracted when using only MNPs (the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs remained unchanged). Therefore, MMH plays an essential role in TC extraction, while MNPs are not associated with extraction. In addition, the fluorescence quenching from the system using MNPs@MMH as an adsorbent (∼70 % quenching) was somewhat more prominent than that of the system using only MMH as an adsorbent (∼60 % quenching). This phenomenon may be attributed to the increased surface area of MMH generated on the MNPs than MMH alone. In addition, the separation of the adsorbent MNPs@MMH from the sample solution using a permanent magnet was faster than the separation of MMH alone by centrifugation. Thus, no centrifugation step was needed for adsorbent separation. This experiment suggested that TC can efficiently be extracted by MNPs@MMH with a more convenient procedure than those in the related extraction system and is potentially useful to preconcentrate TC. The parameters that may affect the extraction efficiency were studied and optimized. The results (Fig. S15, Fig. S16) are presented and discussed in the electronic supporting information. A ratio of Al3+ to Mg2+ of 1:3, a volume of MNPs (1.5 % w/v in water) of 300 µL, a concentration of NaOH for MNPs@MMH generation of 12.5 mM, a vortexing time of 30 min, and an incubation time of 10 min provided the best extraction performance and were used as the optimized conditions.

The effect of solution pH on the extraction performance of TC by MNPs@MMH was investigated, and the results are shown in Fig. S17. A difference in the extraction efficiency was not observed when TC was extracted from the solutions with pH values ranging from 5 to 8. Thus, this system is suitable for the extraction of TC from milk samples and water samples from animal husbandry farms. The effect of ionic strength of the TC solution on the extraction efficiency was investigated by increasing the concentration of Na2SO4 in 40 mL of the solution containing 10 µM TC. The results are shown in Fig. S18. The extraction efficiency was not affected by the ionic strength at Na2SO4 concentrations less than 5 % w/v. However, the ionic strength of the solution affected the extraction efficiency when the concentration of Na2SO4 exceeded 5 % w/v. The explanation for this result is that TC was extracted by MNPs@MMH via an ion exchange mechanism. Thus, in the presence of a high ionic strength, the extraction efficiency may be disturbed, which may be a limitation of the proposed method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of the final detection volume
ce:section.ce:para: The volume of the detection solution directly affects the detection sensitivity. Ideally, the smallest volume of the solution will provide the highest detection sensitivity. However, because a spectrofluorometer was used for detection, the volume of the detection solution must be sufficient to fill a standard cuvette. The experiments were performed by extracting TC from an initial solution volume of 40 mL with MNPs@MMH and elution with different final volumes in the range of 3–40 mL. The sensitivity of the method was examined by monitoring the degree of fluorescence quenching. As shown in Fig. 3 , the highest sensitivity was obtained at a final volume of 3 mL. A final volume less than 3 mL was not examined due to the limitation of the volume of the standard cuvette. Therefore, the final volume of 3 mL was chosen as the optimized value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the method toward the detection of TC
ce:section.ce:para: The approach for direct TC detection using fluorescence probes has limited selectivity for TC over other related antibiotics. The reason is that the main structure of the antibiotics is not different, and designing the receptor of the probe to provide specific interactions is challenging. The selectivity of the proposed method for the accurate detection of TC over other antibiotics and possible interfering species in samples was investigated. The results in Fig. 4 (a) and (b) clearly show that the proposed method was excellent for the selective determination of TC over other studied compounds. The fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs was substantially quenched by only TC, while the other studied compounds showed no significant effect on the N,S-CDs fluorescence intensity. The excellent detection selectivity is based on the assistance of the extraction step using MNPs@MMH. In alkaline medium, the carboxylic acid of TC was ionized to form a negatively charged species, which participated in a strong electrostatic interaction with the positive charge of the MMH. Thus, only the negatively charged compounds were selected to be strongly adsorbed on the MNPs@MMH adsorbent. This step eliminated several compounds from TC. Therefore, only the adsorbed compounds were detected by the N,S-CDs. In addition, TC produced broad absorption peaks superimposed on the excitation and emission spectra of the N,S-CDs that caused the IFE phenomenon with the N,S-CDs and resulted in fluorescence quenching.

Notably, the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs was quenched when the studied compounds were directly added into the N,S-CDs due to the overlapping absorption spectra. This overlap is the drawback of fluorescence detection via the IFE mechanism. However, the combination of the simple extraction step with the unique optical characteristic of TC provided a selective assay for TC detection.

The fluorescence quenching of N,S-CDs was investigated after the addition of TC to assess the performance of the proposed sensor. The fluorescence spectra of N,S-CDs in the presence of various TCconcentrations with and without the extraction step are presented in Fig. 4(c) and (d), respectively. According to the results, the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CDs in both systems was reduced with TC. The amount of TC in the system was proportional to its decreasing efficiency. The sensor sensitivities in the two systems, however, were not the same. As shown in Fig. S19, a linear relationship between the fluorescence quenching (F0-F) and TC concentration was observed from 0.80 to 10 µM using the preconcentration approach. F0-F = 80231[TC] + 8705 with a linear regression coefficient (R2) of 0.9985 is the linear equation for the preconcentration system. For TC detection without preconcentration, a linear relationship was observed at 5.45–15 µM. F0-F = 18090[TC] + 9707 with R2 of 0.9901 was determined to be the linear equation. The limits of detection (LODs) of the preconcentrated and ordinary systems were 0.16 and 1.26 μM, respectively. The results revealed that using MNPs@MMH to extract TC significantly improves the sensitivity of TC detection.

TC has been detected using a variety of fluorescence sensors. Table S2 summarizes the probe types, target analytes, samples, sensing range, and sensitivity based on the LOD for each report. Many different fluorescent nanomaterials have been produced to detect TC. Compared to other reported approaches for TC detection that have been published, the suggested sensor has a high selectivity for TC and a low LOD, implying high sensitivity. According to the results, the fluorescence approach based on MNPs@MMH and N,S-CDs developed in this study was as accurate as or better than existing fluorescence methods reported in the literature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application to the analysis of TC concentrations in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: A milk sample and wastewater from the animal husbandry farm were chosen as the real samples to evaluate the feasibility of applying the presented sensor in an actual environment. Additionally, the same samples and spiked samples were investigated using a typical HPLC technique to evaluate the accuracy of the proposed method. The results of these analyses are illustrated in Table 1 . The concentration of TC in both samples was less than the LOD of the method (0.16 µM). However, the sample spiked with 3 and 6 µM TC was detected with the proposed method with an acceptable % recovery of more than 89 % and a %RSD of less than 8.1 %, signifying that this method possesses good accuracy and precision. The good correspondence between the results obtained using our method and the HPLC results confirmed the excellent accuracy of the developed method. These results suggested that the proposed assay can be employed to examine TC concentrations in the complex matrix of a real sample.

An efficient strategy for the detection of trace levels of TC was successfully established. The proposed sensor relied on combining the preconcentration step using MNPs@MMH and detection via a fluorescence assay based on N,S-CDs through an IFE phenomenon. The trace-level TC in the sample solution was simply extracted by the nanocomposite formed between MMH and MNPs (MNPs@MMH), which was easily separated using a permanent magnetic field. A centrifugation step not needed and the extracted TC was eluted from the adsorbent by simply adding a small volume of acetic acid to dissolve the MMH. This method provided a maximum preconcentration factor of 13.33 (from 40.0 mL to 3.0 mL). Moreover, excellent detection sensitivity was achieved by indirectly measuring the emission intensity of the N,S-CDs, which were synthesized by the hydrothermal treatment of the lemonade and cysteamine. The absorption spectrum of TC was superimposed on both the excitation and emission spectra of the N,S-CDs. Thus, the fluorescence intensity of the N,S-CDs was effectively quenched by the concentrated TC that was extracted using the MNPs@MMH. The TC concentration was related to the degree of fluorescence quenching of the N,S-CDs. Moreover, the proposed assay provided good selectivity toward detecting TC over other common antibiotics. In addition, this strategy provided a linear working range of 0.80–10.0 µM, with a lower detection limit of 0.16 µM. Finally, this method detected TC in milk and wastewater samples from an animal husbandry farm with good accuracy and precision.
